text:
During the early part of the 1994 baseball season, many sports fans and baseball players 
noticed that the number of home runs being hit seemed to be unusually large.  Here are the 
data on the number of home runs hit by American and National League teams: 
 
American League   35, 40, 43, 49, 51, 54, 57, 58, 58, 64, 68, 68, 75, 77 
National League   29, 31, 42, 46, 47, 48, 48, 53, 55, 55, 55, 63, 63, 67

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
Construct a back-to-back stemplot to compare the number of home runs hit in the two 
leagues.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text:
Write a few sentences comparing the distributions of home runs in the two leagues. Be 
sure to include a comparison of the medians as part of your discussion.

text:
Data: Minitab printout of summary statistics for 28 test scores 
 
 
a)  IQR = Q3 - Q1 = 84 – 68 = 16 
 
b)  Low Outlier: Q1 – 1.5 * IQR = 68 – 1.5 * 16 = 68 – 24 = 44. Since the lowest value (35) is 
less than 44, it is an outlier. 
 
High Outlier: Q3 + 1.5 * IQR = 84 + 1.5 * 16 = 84 + 24 = 108. Since the highest value (94) 
is not greater than 108, it is not an outlier.

0.5

a) Verify that the area below this density curve is 1. 
b) What proportion of observations in this density curve are less than 0.75? 
c) What proportion of observations in this density curve are greater than 1.5?

In a study of elite distance runners, the mean weight was reported to be 63.1 kilograms (kg), 
with a standard deviation of 4.8 kg. 
 
a) Assuming that the distribution of weights is normal, sketch the density curve of the 
weight distribution, with the horizontal axis marked in kilograms. 
 
Using your sketch and the 6899.7 rule, answer the following: 
 
b) What range represents the middle 68% of runner’s weights? 
c) What percentage of runner’s weights are less than 58.3 kg? 
d) What percentage of runners weighs between 63.1 kg and 72.7 kg?

A lunch stand in the business district has a mean daily gross income of $520 with a standard 
deviation of $50.  Assume that the daily gross income is normally distributed. 
 
a) In what proportion of days is the gross income less than $400? 
 
 
b) In what proportion of days is the gross income more than $600? 
c) In what proportion of days is the gross income between $400 and $600? 
d) What does the gross income need to be for a day to be in the top 10% of all days?

AP Statistics Chapter 2 Practice Test - ANSWERS

b) What proportion of observations in this density curve are less than 0.75? (.375) 
c) What proportion of observations in this density curve are greater than 1.5? (.25)

In a study of elite distance runners, the mean weight was reported to be 63.1 kilograms (kg), 
with a standard deviation of 4.8 kg. 
 
a) Assuming that the distribution of weights is normal, sketch the density curve of the 
weight distribution, with the horizontal axis marked in kilograms. (Sketch) 
 
Using your sketch and the 6899.7 rule, answer the following: 
 
b) What range represents the middle 68% of runner’s weights? (58.3 – 67.9) 
c) What percentage of runner’s weights are less than 58.3 kg? (16%) 
d) What percentage of runners weighs between 63.1 kg and 72.7 kg? (47.5%)

A lunch stand in the business district has a mean daily gross income of $520 with a standard 
deviation of $50.  Assume that the daily gross income is normally distributed. 
 
a) In what proportion of days is the gross income less than $400? (.0082) 
b) In what proportion of days is the gross income more than $600? (.0548) 
c) In what proportion of days is the gross income between $400 and $600? (.9370) 
d) What does the gross income need to be for a day to be in the top 10% of all days? ($584)

conclude that

the correlation should have been r = 0.61

text:
If data set A of (x, y) data has correlation coefficient r = 0.65, and a second data set B has 
correlation r = –0.65, then

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: the points in A exhibit a stronger linear association than B.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: the points in B exhibit a stronger linear association than A.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: neither A nor B has a stronger linear association.

parts.label: (d)
parts.text:
you can’t tell which data set has a stronger linear association without seeing the data or  
 
seeing the scatterplots.

parts.label: (e)
parts.text: a mistake has been made—r cannot be negative.

text:
There is a linear relationship between the number of chirps made by the striped ground cricket and 
the air temperature.  A least-squares fit of some data collected by a biologist gives the model   
 
  = 25.2 + 3.3x,   9 < x < 25,   where x is the number of chirps per minute and   is the estimated 
temperature in degrees Fahrenheit.  What is the estimated increase in temperature that corresponds 
to an increase of 5 chirps per minute?

text: Which of the following relationships is most likely to result in a strong negative correlation?

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: The number of people showering in a college dorm and the water pressure in each shower.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: The outdoor temperature and the number of fans running in non-air-conditioned dorm rooms.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: The comfort rating of a mattress and the number of hours of uninterrupted sleep obtained.

parts.label: (d)
parts.text: The price of a home and its square footage.

parts.label: (e)
parts.text: The fuel efficiency of a car (miles per gallon) and its speed.

performance rating.  Which of statements

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: to

parts.label: (d)
parts.text:

parts.label: (d)

text:
Leonardo da Vinci, the renowned painter, speculated that an ideal human would have an armspan 
(distance from outstretched fingertip of left hand to outstretched fingertip of right hand) that was 
equal to his height.  The following computer regression printout shows the results of a least-squares 
regression on height and armspan, in inches, for a sample of 18 high school students. 
   
 
 
 
 
Which of the following statements is false?

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
This least-squares regression model would make a prediction that is 1.63 inches higher  
 
 
than da Vinci projected for a 62-inch tall student.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text:
One of the students in the sample had a height of 70.5 inches and an armspan of 68 inches.  The 
residual for this student is 1.83 inches.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text:
Da Vinci’s projection is lower than the prediction that this least-squares line will make for any 
height.

parts.label: (d)
parts.text:
For every one-inch increase in armspan, the regression model predicts about a 0.84-inch  
 
 
increase in height.

parts.label: (e)
parts.text:
For a student 66 inches tall, our model would predict an armspan of about 67 inches. 
Chapter 3 
2 
Test 3C

text:
Anthropologists must often estimate from human remains how tall the person was when alive.  
Carla is studying how overall height can be predicted from the length of a leg bone in a group of 36 
living males.  The data show that the bone lengths have mean 45.9 cm and standard deviation 4.2 
cm, the overall heights have mean 172.7 cm and standard deviation 8.14 cm, and the correlation 
between bone length and height is 0.914.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
Determine the equation of the least-squares regression line of height on bone length.  Show 
your work.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Interpret the correlation in the context of this problem.

text:
In general, is correlation a resistant measure of association?                     Explain briefly or give a 
simple example to illustrate. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
I pledge that I have neither given nor received aid on this test. ____________________________

[empty]

Below is a two-way table that describes responses of 120 subjects to a survey in which they were asked, "Do you exercise for at least 30 minutes four or more times per week?" and "What kind of vehicle do you drive?"

| Exercise? | Sedan | SUV | Truck | Total |
|-----------|-------|-----|-------|-------|
| Yes       | 25    | 15  | 12    | 52    |
| No        | 20    | 24  | 24    | 68    |
| Total     | 45    | 39  | 36    | 120   |

Suppose one person from this sample is randomly selected.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: What is the probability that the person selected drives an SUV?

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: What is the probability that the person selected drives either a sedan or a truck?

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: What is the probability that the person is a non-exerciser that drives a sedan?

parts.label: (d)
parts.text: What is the probability that the person is a non-exerciser or drives a sedan?

parts.label: (e)
parts.text: What is the probability that the person selected drives a truck given that they exercise?

text: There are 35 students in Ms. Ortiz’s Calculus class. One day, 24 students turned in their homework and 14 turned in test corrections. Eight of these students turned in both homework and test corrections. Suppose we randomly select a student from the class.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Complete a Venn diagram below so that it describes the chance process involved here. Let H = the event “turned in homework” and C = the event “turned in corrections.”

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Using your Venn diagram, determine the probability that a student did not turn in homework or their test corrections.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: Using your Venn diagram, determine the probability that a student turned in homework given that they turned in test corrections.

text: You have been handed a bag containing four $1 bills and two $5 bills. You will reach in the bag (blindly) and select two bills at random.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Sketch a complete tree diagram to represent the outcomes of this random selection.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: What is the probability that you end up with $6?

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: What is the probability that the two selected bills are the same dollar amount?

text:
Patients receiving artificial knees often experience pain after surgery.  The pain is measured 
on a subjective scale with possible values of 1 to 5.  Assume that X is a random variable 
representing the pain score for a randomly elected patient.  The following table gives part of 
the probability distribution for X. 
   
 
           X  1 
 2 
 3 
 4 
 5     
 
 
     P(X) .1 
.2 
.3 
.3

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Find P(X = 5).

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Find the probability that the pain score is less than 3.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: Find the probability that the pain score is greater than 3.

parts.label: (d)
parts.text: Find the mean µ for this distribution.

text: A quarterback completes 44% of his passes.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
What is the probability that the quarterback throws 3 incomplete passes before he has a 
completion?

parts.label: (b)
parts.text:
What is the probability that the quarterback throws his first completion in no more than 3 
attempts?

parts.label: (c)
parts.text:
How many passes, on average, can the quarterback expect to throw before he completes 
his first pass?

parts.label: (d)
parts.text:
Use two methods to determine the probability that it takes more than 5 attempts before he 
completes a pass.

text:
A headache remedy is said to be 85% effective in curing headaches caused by simple 
nervous tension.  An investigator tests this remedy on 8 randomly selected patients suffering 
from nervous tension.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Find the probability that the remedy works for 7 of the patients.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Find the probability that the remedy works for more than 6 of the patients.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text: Find the probability that the remedy works for less than half of the patients.

parts.label: (d)
parts.text:
What is the expected value for the number of people in the experiment who have success 
with the remedy?

A 2005 report by the American Management Association summarized the results of an 
extensive survey given to 526 randomly selected U.S. businesses. One of the questions asked 
was whether the company had fired any employees for misuse of the Internet while at work. 
The report gave a confidence interval of .229 to .292 for the proportion of all U.S. companies 
that have fired employees for misuse of the Internet while at work.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: What proportion of the businesses in the sample had answered yes to this question?

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: What was the margin of error for this interval?

parts.label: (c)
parts.text:
Explain to someone who does not know anything about statistics why we can’t simply 
say that the answer to part

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: is true for all U.S. businesses.

text:
A random sample of 1100 teenagers (ages 12 to 17) was asked whether they played games 
online; 775 said that they did.

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Verify that the conditions are met here for inference about a population proportion p.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Construct and interpret a 99% confidence interval for the population proportion p.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text:
How large a sample would you need to take to estimate p within 2% at a 99% confidence 
level?  Use ˆp  for the value of p*. Show your work.

text:
Rocky Mountain Airlines Flight 441 flies from Denver to Albuquerque each day at 8:00 am. 
The flight is listed as taking 58 minutes, on average. A random sample of 9 of these flight 
times, rounded to the nearest minute, is given in the table below. 
 
56 
62 
59 
58 
60 
57 
59 
61 
62

parts.label: (a)
parts.text: Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean flight time for Flight 441.

parts.label: (b)
parts.text: Does the interval in

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
give you reason to suspect that the claim of 58 minutes is false? 
Explain.

parts.label: (c)
parts.text:
What concern do you have about the data used here to construct the confidence interval 
in part (a)? Explain.

text:
A certain intelligence test is designed to have a population of scores following a normal 
distribution with a mean score of 100. Below are scores on this intelligence test from 6 
randomly selected undergraduate students from Thorndike University. 
 
110 
118 
110 
122 
110 
150 
 
a) Do these scores suggest that, on average, the population of undergraduates at Thorndike 
University have higher than average intelligence scores? Carry out an appropriate test at 
the 5% level to help answer this question. 
b) What would constitute a Type I error for this test?

A drug manufacturer claims that 9 out of 10 doctors (90%) recommend aspirin for their 
patients with headaches. To test this claim, a random sample of 100 doctors is obtained. Of 
these 100 doctors, 82 indicate that they recommend aspirin.  
 
a) Do these results support the claim of the drug manufacturer? Support your conclusion 
with a test of significance. Use  = .01. 
b) What would constitute a Type II error for this test?

A study of iron deficiency among infants compared samples of infants following different 
feeding regimens.  One group contained breast-fed infants, while the children in another group 
were fed a standard baby formula without any iron supplements.  Here are the results on blood 
hemoglobin levels at 12 months of age. 
 
 
 
 Group 
 
n 
 
x  
 
 s  
    
 Breast-fed  
23 
 
13.3  
1.7 
 
 
 Formula 
 
19 
 
12.4  
1.8

parts.label: (a)
parts.text:
Is there significant evidence that the mean hemoglobin level is higher among breast-fed 
babies?  Give appropriate statistical evidence to support your conclusion. 
 
Do all of the following: 
a. Identify the parameters of interest.  Then state the appropriate hypotheses. 
b. Verify conditions for carrying out a significance test. 
c. Calculate the test statistic and the P-value. 
d. What conclusion would you draw?

text:
An association of Christmas tree growers in Indiana sponsored a sample survey of 500 
randomly selected Indiana households to help improve the marketing of Christmas trees.  
One question the researchers asked was “Did you have a Christmas tree this year?”   
 
Respondents who had a tree during the holiday season were asked whether the tree was 
natural or artificial.  Respondents were also asked if they lived in an urban area or in a rural 
area.  Of the 421 households displaying a Christmas tree, 160 lived in rural areas and 261 
were urban residents.  Here are the data: 
 
Population  n  
 
X(natural) 
1 (rural)  160   
64 
2 (urban)  261   
89 
 
Do these data provide evidence of a significant difference in the proportion of rural and 
urban Indiana residents who had a natural Christmas tree this year? Perform an appropriate 
test to answer this question. 
a. Identify the parameters of interest.  Then state the appropriate hypotheses. 
b. Verify conditions for carrying out a significance test. 
c. Calculate the test statistic and the P-value. 
d. What conclusion would you draw? 
.

In the 2001 regular baseball season, the World Series Champion Arizona Diamondbacks 
played 81 games at home and 81 games away.  They won 48 of their home games and 44 of 
the games played away.  We can consider these games as samples from potentially large 
populations of games played at home and away.   
 
a. Identify the populations and parameters of interest. 
b. Construct and interpret a 90% confidence interval for the difference between the 
proportion of games that the Diamondbacks win at home and the proportion that they 
win when on the road. 
c. Most people think that it is easier to win at home than away.  Use the confidence 
interval from part b to determine whether this is true for the Arizona Diamondbacks.

Computer software generated 500 random numbers that should look like they are from the 
uniform distribution on the interval 0 to 1.  They are categorized into five groups: (1) less 
than or equal to 0.2 (2) greater than 0.2 and less than or equal to 0.4, (3) greater than 0.4 and 
less than or equal to 0.6, (4) greater than 0.6 and less than or equal to 0.8, and (5) greater than 
0.8.  The counts in the five groups are 113, 95, 108, 99, and 85, respectively.  
 
a. The probabilities for these five intervals are all the same.  What is this probability?   
b. Compute the expected count for each interval for a sample of 500.   
c.  Perform the goodness of fit test and summarize your results. Report the 2 statistic, the P-
value and write an appropriate conclusion.

Here are the hypotheses we will test: 
Ho: Mall shoppers have the same blood type proportions as the general public 
Ha: Mall shoppers DO NOT have the same blood type proportions as the general public

A study of the career plans of young women and men sent questionnaires to all 390 members 
of the senior class in the College of Business Administration at the University of Illinois.  
One question asked which major within the business program the student had chosen. Here 
are the data from the students who responded: 
Major/Gender 
Female 
Male 
Accounting

Administration 
91 
40 
Economics

This is an example of a single sample classified according to two categorical variables 
(gender and major).  
.  
a. Verify that the expected cell counts satisfy the requirement for use of chi-square.   
b. Compute the expected count for the female economics cell of the table. SHOW WORK.   
c.  Test the null hypothesis that there is no relationship between the gender of students and 
their choice of major.  Give the 2 statistic and the P-value. 
d. State your conclusion in a sentence or two.

AP Statistics – Chapter 11 Practice FR Solutions

Goodness of Fit Test – Equal Proportions 
a) Since there are 5 groups, the probability is 1/5 = 0.2 
b) Since there are 500 numbers generated, expected counts are 500(0.2) = 500/5 = 100 
c) The 
2 statistic is 4.84. There are 4 degrees of freedom here, so the p-value is .3041. 
 
Conclusion: Since the p-value is high (p>.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. So we can 
conclude that the random numbers are uniformly distributed among the 5 groups.

d) Since the p-value is less than .05, we reject the null hypothesis. So we can conclude that 
there is a relationship between gender and their choice of business major. Stated another 
way, men and women are different in their choices of business majors.

In a study of heart surgery, one issue was the effect of drugs called beta-blockers on the pulse 
rate of patients during surgery.  The available subjects were divided at random into two 
groups of 30 patients each.  One group received a beta-blocker; the other group received a 
placebo.  The pulse rate of each patient at a critical point during the operation was recorded.  
The treatment group had mean 65.2 and standard deviation 7.8.  For the control group, the 
mean was 70.3 and the standard deviation was 8.3. 
 
Perform an appropriate significance test to see if beta-blockers reduce the pulse rate. 
a. Identify the populations and parameters of interest.  Then state hypotheses. 
b. State and verify conditions for carrying out a significance test. 
c. Calculate the test statistic and the P-value. 
d. What conclusion would you draw?

A study of “adverse symptoms” in users of over-the-counter pain relief medications assigned 
subjects at random to one of two common pain relievers: acetaminophen and ibuprofen.  In 
all, 650 subjects took acetaminophen, and 44 experienced some adverse symptom.  Of the 
347 subjects who took ibuprofen, 49 had an adverse symptom.  We want to assess the 
evidence that the two pain relievers differ in the proportion of people who experience an 
adverse symptom. 
 
Perform an appropriate significance test to answer the question posed. 
a. Identify the populations and parameters of interest.  Then state hypotheses. 
b. State and verify conditions for carrying out a significance test. 
c. Calculate the test statistic and the P-value. 
d. What conclusion would you draw? 
.

win when on the road. 
c. Most people think that it is easier to win at home than away.  Use the confidence 
interval from part b to determine whether this is true for the Arizona Diamondbacks.
